title: Order saga example with compensating rollbacks
purpose: Sagas with compensating rollbacks are the bread-and-butter of durable workflow engines. Developers adopting Aion need a concrete, copy-pasteable example showing how to sequence activities (reserve_inventory, charge_payment, ship_order), detect failures at any step, and execute compensating actions in reverse order (cancel_shipment, refund_payment, release_inventory). Without this example, every team will reinvent the saga pattern incorrectly.
task: Create a complete order-saga example that defines a multi-step workflow in Gleam using the aion_flow SDK. The workflow runs three activities in sequence — reserve_inventory, charge_payment, ship_order — and if any step fails, runs the corresponding compensating activities in reverse order. Include a Python worker implementing the activity stubs, a gleam.toml with the aion_flow dependency, and a README explaining the saga pattern and how to run the example.

requirements.title: Saga workflow with sequential activities and compensation
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a workflow using workflow.define() that executes reserve_inventory, charge_payment, and ship_order activities in sequence using activity.new(). WHEN any activity fails, THE SYSTEM SHALL execute the compensating activities for all previously completed steps in reverse order.
acceptance: order_saga.gleam imports from aion_flow and uses workflow.define().
Three forward activities are declared with activity.new() and executed in sequence.
At least three compensating activities (release_inventory, refund_payment, cancel_shipment) are declared.
On charge_payment failure, release_inventory runs. On ship_order failure, refund_payment then release_inventory run.
The workflow returns Ok on full success and Error with compensation details on failure.

requirements.title: Python worker with activity stubs
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a Python worker that registers all six activities (three forward, three compensating) using the aion-worker SDK. Each activity SHALL accept typed input and return typed output suitable for chaining.
acceptance: worker.py registers reserve_inventory, charge_payment, ship_order, release_inventory, refund_payment, and cancel_shipment.
Each activity accepts JSON input and returns JSON output.
At least one activity can be configured to simulate failure for testing the compensation path.
The worker starts and connects to the Aion server.

requirements.title: Project setup and documentation
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a gleam.toml with the aion_flow dependency and a README explaining the saga pattern, the compensation strategy, and step-by-step instructions for building the .aion archive and running the example end-to-end.
acceptance: gleam.toml lists aion_flow as a dependency and has correct project metadata.
README.md explains the saga pattern and why compensating actions are needed.
README.md includes a sequence diagram or textual flow showing the happy path and compensation path.
README.md provides build and run instructions (gleam build, .aion packaging, worker startup, workflow trigger).

boundaries: SHALL NOT modify the aion_flow SDK, engine, or server code — only consume them.
SHALL NOT add dependencies beyond aion_flow to the Gleam example.
SHALL NOT implement real payment or inventory integrations — activity stubs with simulated behaviour are sufficient.
SHALL NOT introduce retry logic within the saga — compensation is the error-handling strategy demonstrated here.
verification: gleam build succeeds in the order-saga directory without warnings.
The .aion package can be built from the compiled source.
The Python worker starts and registers all six activities without errors.
The happy-path workflow completes successfully with all three forward activities executed in order.
When charge_payment is configured to fail, release_inventory runs as compensation.
When ship_order is configured to fail, refund_payment and release_inventory run in reverse order.
README instructions are complete and accurate.
